---
title: "Appendix D — Calculation in Kind: Neurath's Naturalrechnung"
description: "The technical apparatus behind Otto Neurath's argument that planning can proceed without scalar reduction — multi-criteria optimization, Pareto frontiers, and the ecological-economics revival."
---

## Why this appendix exists

Otto Neurath (1882–1945) is the figure Mises is replying to in 1920. His argument — that an industrial economy can be coordinated in physical units without reduction to a common monetary scalar — appears in Controversy 2 (as the chapter's opener and as Version C of the mini-project), in Week 8 (as the multi-resource LP variant of the planning problem), and in Controversy 8 (as the lineage behind the dual labor-/CO₂-accounting framework). This appendix is the single place where the *technical apparatus* is treated in depth: the multi-criteria optimization formalism, the Pareto-frontier construction, the scalarization choices, and the historical and modern context that makes *Naturalrechnung* a serious contemporary planning tradition rather than a curiosity. Read it in support of any of those three chapters; nothing here is on the critical path of the main spine.

## Historical context

Neurath served as head of the Central Economic Office of the Munich Soviet Republic in spring 1919 — an unusually direct case of an academic economist becoming the man with the actual job of "draft a plan for socialization of an industrial economy by next Tuesday." The Bavarian socialization scheme he co-authored, and the parallel work on the Saxon scheme, were the immediate institutional context for *Durch die Kriegswirtschaft zur Naturalwirtschaft* (1919). Neurath's claim that an in-kind economy was already a proven concept rested on the wartime German planning apparatus — the *Kriegsrohstoffabteilung* (War Raw Materials Department, set up under Walther Rathenau in 1914) and the *Reichsamt für die Volksernährung* (Reich Food Office) had coordinated steel, coal, grain, and fertilizer flows across a four-year industrial war without using market prices in the central allocation decisions. Neurath read this not as a wartime aberration but as a demonstration that scalar monetary reduction is a convenience, not a logical necessity.

After the Munich Soviet was crushed and Neurath imprisoned, he moved through Vienna's "Red Vienna" municipal-socialist period — the largest experiment in democratic-socialist municipal administration in interwar Europe — where his work on *Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum* (the Vienna Social and Economic Museum, 1925) developed the visual-statistics method that would become *Isotype* (the International System of Typographic Picture Education). The Isotype project is plausibly the most underrated piece of the Vienna intellectual world; it is a serious technology for *communicating planning information* to a non-specialist public, and it deserves a place on any reading ladder for "computational economics for democratic publics." It is mentioned again at the end of this appendix.

The economic-calculation work runs through *Wirtschaftsplan und Naturalrechnung* (1925) and the related papers collected in Uebel & Cohen, *Otto Neurath: Economic Writings 1904–1945* (Kluwer 2004) — the canonical English-language collection. The Vienna Circle / *Wiener Kreis* connection is real and deep: Neurath was a founding member, the principal organizer, and the author (with Carnap and Hahn) of the 1929 manifesto *Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung*. His logical-empiricist philosophy of science and his economic-planning philosophy share a common commitment — to publicly verifiable, intersubjective, theory-light description of the physical world — which is what makes *Naturalrechnung* internally consistent rather than ad hoc. (Neurath's "boat" metaphor — that we rebuild science plank by plank while still afloat — is from the same period and the same intellectual stance.)

## The technical core: planning as multi-criteria optimization

The heart of Neurath's economics is the claim that comparing alternative production plans is structurally a *multi-criteria* problem: each plan is a vector of physical magnitudes (so much labor, so much energy, so much land of various qualities, so much steel, so much grain, so much CO₂, so much occupational health, so much consumption of each kind), and rational choice among such vectors does not require — and in many cases is *distorted by* — projection onto a single scalar. Modern operations research has developed this into a substantial subfield: *multi-objective optimization*, *vector optimization*, *goal programming* (Charnes-Cooper-Ferguson 1955 onward), *Pareto-efficiency theory*. Neurath got the conceptual move right four decades before the mathematics was systematized; what we have now is the technical machinery to do what he was already arguing was the right kind of thing to do.

### Lebenslagen as vectors

A *Lebenslage* (life-situation) is Neurath's term for the multi-dimensional state of a household, region, or society: a tuple of physical magnitudes describing food, shelter, work-time, leisure, health, education, environment. A plan is, in this language, a choice of social *Lebenslage* — a vector in a high-dimensional commodity-and-quality space. The planning question is not "what is the maximum value this society can produce?" (a scalar question) but "which *Lebenslagen* are feasible and which among them is preferable?" (a vector question). The latter has no logical answer until one says *which dimensions matter and at what trade-off rates* — which is a political-deliberative question, not an economic-technical one.

### Comparison without scalar reduction: the formal options

Given two feasible plans $x_A, x_B \in \mathbb{R}^k_+$ (with $k$ physical dimensions), there are several rigorous senses in which one can be "better":

1. **Pareto dominance.** $x_A \succ x_B$ iff $x_{A,i} \geq x_{B,i}$ for all $i$ and strictly for at least one. This gives a *partial* order: many pairs are incomparable. The set of Pareto-undominated plans is the *Pareto frontier* (or *efficient frontier*). Comparison within the frontier requires further choice.
2. **Lexicographic ordering.** Rank dimensions by political priority; compare on the highest-priority dimension first, break ties by the next, etc. This gives a total order *given a priority ranking*. The political content is fully concentrated in the choice of priority ranking.
3. **Scalarization (weighted sum).** Choose weights $w \in \mathbb{R}^k_+$, define $f(x) = \sum_i w_i x_i$, optimize. This recovers a single optimum but only by smuggling in a price-like exchange rate $w_i / w_j$ between every pair of dimensions. Sweeping $w$ over the simplex traces out (under convexity) the entire Pareto frontier — which is exactly what the Version C mini-project in Controversy 2 does.
4. **ε-constraint method.** Optimize one dimension as the objective and treat the others as constraints with bounds $\varepsilon_i$; sweep the bounds to trace the frontier. This is Neurath's procedure rendered in modern LP: each "important" physical dimension gets a constraint, and the planner chooses one dimension to maximize and politically-set bounds on the rest. Practically dominant in modern multi-objective LP because it avoids the convexity assumption that weighted-sum requires for full frontier coverage.

The Mises 1920 critique reads, in this light, as the claim that *only* scalarization yields a unique answer, and that the political-deliberative choice required by partial-order, lexicographic, and ε-constraint methods is not "calculation" but politics. The Neurath-O'Neill reply is that this is correct — the scalarization choice is irreducibly political — and that pretending otherwise (by treating market prices as the politically-neutral scalarization) is the actual error.

## The Mises 1920 reply, in Neurath's terms

What Mises is and is not refuting becomes much clearer once Neurath's position is named. Mises is *not* refuting:

- The empirical claim that wartime German planning worked. (He concedes the wartime case was unusually constrained but does not challenge that it functioned.)
- The technical claim that physical-unit accounting is feasible. (He concedes this; large firms did it internally.)
- The claim that consumer goods can have prices in a planned economy. (He explicitly allows this throughout.)

Mises *is* refuting:

- The claim that physical-unit accounting suffices for *rational* choice between alternative production plans for the means of production. His argument is that without a common unit, you cannot compare a plan that uses 100 tons of steel and 50 hours of labor with a plan that uses 80 tons of steel and 70 hours of labor *unless* you have an exchange rate between steel and labor — and only a market in the means of production produces such an exchange rate.

The Neurathian reply: there is no logically privileged exchange rate between steel and labor, and there is no logically privileged exchange rate between any two physical magnitudes; the choice of exchange rate is a political-deliberative act. The market produces *an* exchange rate, and a stable one given certain institutional conditions, but not a logically privileged one. The Mises argument therefore proves the weaker claim that *rational choice without scalarization is incomplete*, but not the stronger claim that *scalarization must come from market prices*. Lange's tâtonnement and Cockshott's labor-time accept the weaker Mises claim and propose substitute scalarizations (planner-set tâtonnement prices; embodied labor); Neurath rejects the weaker claim and treats the political-deliberative choice as the system's defining feature, not a defect.

Hayek 1945 partly retreats to information-economics ground because the calculation ground is harder to defend against Neurath than the Austrian narrative typically admits. The 1945 paper's argument is genuinely different from Mises 1920 (as the chapter Day 3 reading emphasizes), and one way to read the difference is as a tactical retreat: Neurath had been institutionally serious about *Naturalrechnung* in Vienna throughout the 1920s, and the calculation-impossibility claim had not foreclosed it. Hayek shifts the argument to the dispersed-tacit-knowledge ground, where the case against planning is stronger and not vulnerable to Neurath's "the war proved it works at industrial scale" reply.

## The ecological-economics revival

K. William Kapp's *The Social Costs of Private Enterprise* (1950) is the bridge between Neurath's interwar work and the modern ecological-economics tradition. Kapp's central argument — that the costs of industrial production fall systematically on third parties, and that no monetary aggregation can correctly internalize them because the costs are *qualitatively heterogeneous* — is *Naturalrechnung* applied to environmental damage. Joan Martinez-Alier's *Ecological Economics: Energy, Environment and Society* (Blackwell 1987), particularly Chapter 11 ("Otto Neurath and the Calculation Debates"), is the canonical reconstruction of the Neurath–Kapp lineage as the foundation of ecological economics. Martinez-Alier's later work on *incommensurability of values* (with Munda, O'Neill, and others) makes the technical claim explicit: environmental decisions are intrinsically multi-criteria and cannot be reduced to a monetary scalar without distortion.

John O'Neill is the contemporary theorist who has done the heaviest work of grounding ecological economics in Neurath. His relevant texts:

- "In Partial Praise of a Positivist: The Work of Otto Neurath" (*Radical Philosophy* 74, 1995) — short, free, the best entry point.
- *The Market: Ethics, Knowledge, and Politics* (Routledge 1998), Chapter 8 — book-length treatment.
- "Socialist Calculation and Environmental Valuation: Money, Markets and Ecology" (*Science & Society* 66:1, 2002) — directly addresses the calculation debate from a Neurathian-ecological standpoint.
- *Markets, Deliberation and Environment* (Routledge 2007), Chapter 4 — Neurath as a planning theorist for the ecological era.

Recent uses include Clive Spash's work on incommensurability, Kohei Saito's *Marx in the Anthropocene* (2023), and the multi-objective LP framework Vettese & Pendergrass deploy in *Half-Earth Socialism* (2022). The climate-planning LP you build in Controversy 8 — labor-time and CO₂ as parallel accounting dimensions, no enforced reduction to a single number — is a small, working instance of the Neurath–O'Neill apparatus.

## A worked example: a three-sector, three-resource Pareto frontier

Build a small economy: three sectors, three physical resources (labor, energy, steel-equivalent), one consumption-scale objective. The technical matrix $A$ and the resource-coefficient vectors $v$ (labor), $e$ (energy), $s$ (steel) are given. Final-demand composition $\beta$ is given. The multi-criteria planning problem is:

$$
\max \alpha \quad \text{s.t.} \quad (I - A) x \geq \alpha \beta, \quad v \cdot x \leq L, \quad e \cdot x \leq E, \quad s \cdot x \leq S, \quad x \geq 0.
$$

Sweeping the resource bounds $(L, E, S)$ over a grid traces the Pareto frontier in $(\alpha, L\text{-use}, E\text{-use}, S\text{-use})$ space. The frontier is a 3-D surface (after fixing $\alpha$ at each point) and admits direct visualization. The exercise — left as a stretch goal for any reader who works the appendix — is to implement this in cvxpy and produce an interactive Plotly or JSXGraph widget that lets the reader sweep the resource bounds and watch the optimum slide across the frontier. This is the appendix's payoff: the reader sees with their eyes that *the optimum is not unique until the political choice is made*. Cockshott's three-sector example in *Towards a New Socialism* (the energy / food / machines economy with multiple land qualities) is structurally already such a frontier-tracing exercise, but the Neurath–O'Neill framing makes the point about scalarization choice explicit where Cockshott's narrative does not.

## Open theoretical questions

Honest treatment, not a victory lap. *Naturalrechnung* has real open problems, and the recent ecological-economics literature has not closed them.

1. **The aggregation problem.** A frontier in three or four dimensions is visualizable; a frontier in a hundred dimensions is not. At what point does the multi-criteria approach degrade from "richer than monetary scalarization" to "computationally and cognitively unworkable"? The standard answer is "use a small number of high-priority dimensions and treat the rest as constraints," but this is itself a hierarchy choice that requires political justification.
2. **The deliberation problem.** What political procedure picks a frontier point? Is it economic theory's job to specify the procedure? The classical Neurath answer (in the 1920s context) was workers' councils and parliamentary deliberation; the modern O'Neill answer involves deliberative-democracy theory (Habermas, Dryzek). Either way, the procedure is *not* derivable from the LP itself.
3. **The information problem.** Does Neurathian planning escape Hayek 1945 or just relocate the difficulty? The honest answer is: it relocates it. The dispersed-tacit-knowledge problem applies to *any* central allocator regardless of whether the allocator uses monetary or physical units. What Neurath's framework adds is that the relocation is *visible* — one cannot disguise the political-deliberative content of the planning choice as an economic-technical answer.
4. **The dynamics problem.** Most of the formalism above is static. Real economies move. How does multi-criteria optimization combine with intertemporal accumulation, learning, and technical change? Modern multi-objective optimal control exists but is harder than its single-objective counterpart, and the Neurath tradition has not fully digested the dynamics literature. Open territory for the curious reader.

## Isotype: a closing note

Neurath's *Wiener Methode der Bildstatistik* — the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics, later renamed Isotype — was an attempt to develop a publicly legible visual language for economic and social statistics. The premise was that democratic-socialist planning is meaningless if its planning information is legible only to specialists, so the planning apparatus must include a serious technology for *communicating physical-magnitude data to a non-specialist public*. Isotype is recognizably the ancestor of modern infographic and dashboard design (and of the entire field of information visualization, by lineage through Marie Neurath, Rudolf Modley, and the post-WWII American visualization tradition). Note it. There is a real argument that any contemporary planning project should ship with an Isotype-style public-communication layer — not a polish-the-research-paper afterthought but a constitutive part of the planning system. This is well off the mathematical spine of the Calculation Course but on the spine of any larger democratic-planning project the course might inform. Marie Neurath & Robin Kinross, *The Transformer: Principles of Making Isotype Charts* (Hyphen Press 2009) is the standard modern reconstruction; Christopher Burke et al., *Isotype: Design and Contexts 1925–1971* (Hyphen Press 2013) is the deeper history.
